key_themes: Private equity acquisition by Sycamore Partners and transition to private ownership
Aggressive cost-cutting including hundreds of layoffs (600+ in IL/TX) and benefit reductions
Accelerated store and distribution center closures as part of restructuring
Ambitious turnaround targeting ~$4B EBITDA (doubling prior levels) via efficiencies, pricing, and new products
Broader retail pharmacy challenges (competition, margins, theft) and criticism of PE practices
summary: Walgreens Boots Alliance was acquired by Sycamore Partners in August 2025 (~$10B deal) and taken private, with the business being split into standalone units under new CEO Mike Motz. Recent news is dominated by cost-cutting—over 600 layoffs announced in early 2026, distribution center closures, reduced benefits, and continued store rationalization (hundreds already closed toward a larger target)—which has drawn strong criticism from labor groups and PE watchdogs as a familiar pattern that harms workers and communities.[[1]](https://pestakeholder.org/media_coverage/walgreens-layoffs-confirm-concerns-after-private-equity-buyout/)[[2]](https://www.al.com/news/2026/02/national-chain-announces-major-job-cuts-affecting-more-than-600-employees-in-2-states.html)[[1]](https://pestakeholder.org/media_coverage/walgreens-layoffs-confirm-concerns-after-private-equity-buyout/) While the company is pursuing a credible path to double EBITDA to ~$4B through operational improvements and better reimbursement rates, public and social sentiment remains negative amid the restructuring pain and lack of ongoing public equity discussion.[[3]](https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/stocks/articles/walgreens-targets-4-billion-ebitda-204641300.html)
sources: 15+ recent news articles (Bloomberg, Reuters, Yahoo Finance, Healthcare Dive, PESP reports on 2026 layoffs and acquisition), Limited relevant X/social volume (dozens of $WBA mentions, mostly low-engagement spam, soccer team references, or older PE criticism; few active investor discussions post-delisting)
